🏔️ EverestOS Documentation

Welcome to the official documentation for EverestOS — a premium, modular web-based desktop environment born in Nepal.

📦 App Catalog 🖥️ User Guide 🛠️ Developer Guide

✨ Platform Features

⚡ Native Performance

Built with vanilla JavaScript and modern web standards — no heavy frameworks, maximum speed.

🔒 Sandboxed Execution

Every application runs in isolation. Extensions use a controlled CommonJS runtime environment.

🎨 Adaptive Theming

Full light/dark mode with 600+ high-fidelity Bloom SVG icons that adapt to your theme automatically.

💾 Virtual File System

A complete filesystem abstraction supporting IndexedDB and native Node.js backends.

🧩 Extension System

Cinnamon-compatible applets and desklets powered by a custom CJS loader and mock St libraries.

🛠️ Developer Tools

Built-in Developer Center, Looking Glass console (Alt+F2), and live code editor for rapid prototyping.

📦 Built-in Application Catalog

EverestOS ships with 18 built-in applications, each designed for performance and visual consistency.

System & Administration

📁 Files System

Full-featured file manager inspired by Nemo. Browse, copy, move, rename, and trash files with keyboard shortcuts.

💻 Terminal System

Command-line shell with built-in commands like ls, cd, cat, mkdir, rm, and more.

⚙️ System Settings System

Centralized configuration hub — appearance, themes, display scaling, night light, panel position, and About page.

🛒 App Center Administration

Discover, install, and uninstall applications. Browse curated apps and manage your software library.

🧩 Plugin Settings System

Manage applets, desklets, and system extensions. Enable, disable, configure, and remove plugins.

📊 System Inspector System

View system statistics, loaded plugins, extension errors, and performance information.

⚙️ Desktop Settings Preferences

Configure desktop wallpaper, icon visibility, and desktop-specific appearance options.

Productivity & Office

📝 Text Editor Accessories

Lightweight text and code editor with Markdown preview support.

💼 Office Office

Professional document editor with rich formatting and cinematic design.

📕 PDF Reader Office

Read and navigate PDF documents within the desktop environment.

🧮 Calculator Utilities

Simple mathematical calculator for everyday calculations.

📦 Archive Manager Accessories

View and extract ZIP archives directly in the file system.

Multimedia

🖼️ Image Viewer Graphics

View images and photos with zoom, rotate, and fullscreen support.

🎵 Music Player Multimedia

Audio player for your music files with playback controls.

🎬 Video Player Multimedia

Watch video files with standard playback controls.

🎬 Media Viewer Media

Universal media viewer for images, videos, and audio files.

Internet & Development

🌐 Web Browser Internet

Embedded web browser for accessing websites and web applications. Also used to view internal documentation.

💻 Developer Center Development

Integrated development environment for creating Apps, Desklets, and Applets. Export your creations to the VFS.

🧩 System Extensions

Beyond standalone apps, EverestOS supports three types of extensions that integrate directly into the desktop shell — modeled after the Cinnamon Desktop Environment.

📌 Applets — Panel Widgets

Small tools that live in the taskbar panel. Examples: clocks, date displays, system monitors, and the main application menu.

Location: ~/Plugins/applets/<uuid>/

🖥️ Desklets — Desktop Widgets

Floating, draggable widgets on the desktop surface. Examples: seven-segment clocks, calendar displays, system info panels.

Location: ~/Plugins/desklets/<uuid>/

📱 User Applications

Custom applications installed by the user or through the App Center. These appear in the App Menu and can be uninstalled.

Location: ~/Apps/<app-name>/

📥 How to Install & Manage Apps

Installing

  • Open the App Center from the App Menu
  • Browse the Curated or Installed tabs
  • Click Install on any application card
  • The app will appear in your App Menu immediately

Uninstalling

  • Open the App Center and go to the Installed tab
  • Click Uninstall on any user-installed application
  • Built-in system applications cannot be uninstalled

Managing Extensions

  • Open Plugin Settings from the App Menu or System Settings
  • Toggle extensions on or off with the enable/disable controls
  • Right-click any applet or desklet for context menu options (Settings, About, Remove, Reload)

💡 Creating Your Own Apps

Use the Developer Center to create, edit, and export custom applications. Each app needs just two files: app.json (manifest) and app.js (entry point). See the Developer Guide for full API documentation.

🚧 Coming Soon

The App Center install functionality for remote/community apps currently operates as a UI preview. A centralized repository server for one-click community app installations is planned for a future release.

EverestOS Developer Guides

Welcome to the official development documentation for EverestOS — a premium, modular web-based desktop environment born in Nepal. This guide covers building applications, extensions, and working with the system APIs.

📦 App Catalog 🖥️ User Guide 🛠️ Developer Guide

🌿 Inspired by Cinnamon Desktop

EverestOS draws heavy inspiration from the Cinnamon Desktop Environment by the Linux Mint team. We've ported the core extension concepts — Applets, Desklets, and the Settings Schema system — to the web using a custom CommonJS loader and mock St (Shell Toolkit) libraries.

📑 Table of Contents

  1. Building Applications
  2. Building Applets (Panel Widgets)
  3. Building Desklets (Desktop Widgets)
  4. Virtual File System API
  5. Window Manager API
  6. Icon Helper API
  7. Theme System
  8. System Dialogs
  9. Configuration Files
  10. Keyboard Shortcuts

1. Building Applications

Applications are the primary extension mechanism in EverestOS. Each app is a self-contained module with a manifest and entry point.

App Directory Structure

~/Apps/my-app/
├── app.json # Manifest (required)
└── app.js # Entry point (required)

Manifest (app.json)

{
  "id": "my-app",
  "name": "My Application",
  "description": "A sample EverestOS application",
  "icon": "archive",
  "category": "Utilities",
  "version": "1.0.0"
}
FieldRequiredDescription
id✅Unique identifier (kebab-case)
name✅Display name shown in App Menu
description❌Short description
icon❌Bloom theme icon key or emoji (e.g., "folder", "📦")
category❌Menu category: Utilities, Development, System, Multimedia, Internet, Office
version❌Semantic version string

Entry Point (app.js)

Your app must export an async launch function that receives the system context:

export async function launch(ctx, options = {}) {
  const { windowManager, vfs, appLoader } = ctx;

  // Create your UI
  const content = document.createElement('div');
  content.style.cssText = 'padding:20px; color:var(--text-primary);';
  content.innerHTML = '<h1>Hello EverestOS!</h1>';

  // Open a window
  windowManager.createWindow({
    id: 'my-app-' + Date.now(),
    title: 'My Application',
    icon: 'archive',
    width: 600,
    height: 400,
    content
  });
}

Context Object Properties

PropertyTypeDescription
windowManagerWindowManagerCreate, close, minimize, and focus windows
vfsVirtualFileSystemRead/write the virtual filesystem
appLoaderAppLoaderLaunch other applications programmatically
panelManagerPanelManagerAccess the taskbar and system tray
filePickerFilePickerAppOpen file/folder selection dialogs
consoleLookingGlassLog messages to the Looking Glass console
themeManagerThemeManagerGet current theme information
showSystemDialogFunctionDisplay alert/confirm dialogs

💡 Launching Other Apps

You can launch any registered app from your code: ctx.appLoader.launchApp('web-browser', { url: 'https://example.com' })

2. Building Applets Panel

Applets are panel widgets that use the Cinnamon-compatible CommonJS pattern with require().

Applet Structure

~/Plugins/applets/my-applet@author/
├── metadata.json # Extension metadata
├── applet.js # Entry point
└── settings-schema.json # Optional settings

Example Applet

const Applet = require('ui/applet');
const St = require('gi/st');
const Main = require('ui/main');

class MyApplet extends Applet.TextIconApplet {
  _init(orientation, panelHeight, instanceId) {
    super._init(orientation, panelHeight, instanceId);
    this.set_applet_icon_name('preferences-system');
    this.set_applet_label('Hello');
    this.set_applet_tooltip('My custom applet');
  }

  on_applet_clicked() {
    Main.notify('Applet clicked!');
  }
}

function main(metadata, orientation, panelHeight, instanceId) {
  return new MyApplet(orientation, panelHeight, instanceId);
}

St.IconIcon display (supports theme keys)

3. Building Desklets Desktop

Desklets are floating desktop widgets. They can be dragged, configured, and persist across sessions.

const Desklet = require('ui/desklet');
const St = require('gi/st');

class MyDesklet extends Desklet.Desklet {
  _init(metadata, instanceId) {
    super._init(metadata, instanceId);
    this.setupUI();
  }

  setupUI() {
    const label = new St.Label({
      text: 'Hello Desktop!',
      style: 'font-size: 24px; color: white;'
    });
    this.setContent(label);
  }

  on_desklet_removed() {
    // Cleanup timers, intervals, etc.
  }
}

function main(metadata, instanceId) {
  return new MyDesklet(metadata, instanceId);
}

4. Virtual File System API

The VFS provides a unified filesystem interface that works in both development (Node.js backend) and production (IndexedDB) modes.

const vfs = ctx.vfs;

// Read directory contents
const items = await vfs.readdir('/home/user/Documents');

// Read a file
const text = await vfs.readFile('~/Documents/notes.txt');

// Write a file
await vfs.writeFile('~/Documents/notes.txt', 'Hello!');

// Check if path exists
const stat = await vfs.stat('~/Documents');

// Create a directory
await vfs.mkdir('~/Documents/new-folder');

// Copy, rename, delete
await vfs.copy('~/file.txt', '~/Desktop/file.txt');
await vfs.rename('~/old.txt', '~/new.txt');
await vfs.rm('~/temp.txt');
await vfs.trash('~/unwanted.txt');

📁 Path Aliases

~ resolves to /home/user. All paths are absolute within the VFS namespace.

5. Window Manager API

const win = windowManager.createWindow({
  id: 'unique-window-id',
  title: 'My Window',
  icon: 'folder',
  width: 600,
  height: 400,
  content: myDomElement
});

// Close, focus, or minimize
windowManager.closeWindow('unique-window-id');
windowManager.focusWindow('unique-window-id');
windowManager.minimizeWindow('unique-window-id');

6. Icon Helper API

Always use IconHelper for theme-aware icon rendering:

// Theme icon with emoji fallback
IconHelper.getIcon('folder,📁', { size: 24 });

// Fixed path (ignores theme)
IconHelper.getIcon('/icons/everest-logo.svg', { size: 64 });

Icon keys use comma-separated format: 'bloom-theme-key,emoji-fallback'

7. Theme System

Themes are JSON files in public/themes/ that define CSS variables and icon packs:

{
  "name": "Mint Dark",
  "type": "dark",
  "icon-theme": "bloom-dark",
  "colors": {
    "--bg-primary": "#1a1a2e",
    "--text-primary": "#e8e8e8",
    "--mint-green": "#6ecf8a"
  }
}

Key CSS variables: --bg-primary, --bg-surface, --bg-card, --text-primary, --text-secondary, --text-accent, --border, --mint-green.

8. System Dialogs

// Alert
ctx.showSystemDialog({
  title: 'Notice',
  message: 'Operation complete.',
  type: 'alert'
});

// Confirm
ctx.showSystemDialog({
  title: 'Confirm',
  message: 'Proceed?',
  type: 'confirm',
  confirmText: 'Yes',
  onConfirm: () => { /* ... */ }
});

🏔️ EverestOS User Guide

This guide covers everything you need to know to use EverestOS effectively — from navigating the desktop to customizing themes and managing your files.

📦 App Catalog 🖥️ User Guide 🛠️ Developer Guide

📑 Contents

  1. The Desktop
  2. The Panel (Taskbar)
  3. App Menu
  4. Window Management
  5. File Management
  6. Themes & Appearance
  7. Keyboard Shortcuts
  8. Extensions & Plugins
  9. Configuration
  10. Debugging (Looking Glass)

1. The Desktop

The desktop is your main workspace. It displays:

  • Desktop icons — Shortcuts to Files, Terminal, App Center, and Trash
  • Desklets — Floating widgets like clocks and calendars
  • A watermark — Shows "EverestOS - Web operating system"

Right-click the desktop to access options like:

  • Add/remove desklets
  • Change desktop settings (wallpaper, icon visibility)
  • Create new folders and files
  • Open the Looking Glass console

💡 Desktop Icon Positions

Desktop icons can be dragged to custom positions. Your layout is automatically saved to ~/.config/desktop-positions.json.

2. The Panel (Taskbar)

The panel sits at the bottom (or top) of the screen and contains three zones:

ZoneContents
LeftApp Menu button, quick-launch applets
CenterWindow list (open application buttons)
RightSystem tray — clock, date, status indicators

Right-click the panel to access:

  • Panel position (move to top or bottom)
  • Add/remove applets
  • Extension manager
  • System settings

3. App Menu

Click the App Menu button (bottom-left of the panel) to launch applications.

  • Quick Actions — Top row has buttons for Files, Terminal, Settings, and About
  • Search — Type to filter apps by name
  • Categories — Browse apps organized by type (System, Multimedia, Office, etc.)
  • All Applications — See everything in one list

4. Window Management

Application windows behave like a traditional desktop:

  • Drag title bar — Move the window
  • Drag edges/corners — Resize the window
  • Close button (✕) — Close the window
  • Minimize button (−) — Minimize to taskbar
  • Click taskbar entry — Restore or focus window
  • Click any window — Bring it to front

5. File Management

The Files app provides a familiar file browser:

  • Navigate — Click folders to open them, use breadcrumb path bar
  • Home (~/) — Your personal directory
  • Desktop (~/Desktop) — Items shown on the desktop
  • Documents, Music, Videos, images — Organized content folders
  • Trash — Deleted items can be restored or permanently removed

File Operations

ActionMethod
Open fileDouble-click (opens in associated app)
Select multipleCtrl+Click or Ctrl+A to select all
CopyRight-click → Copy, or Ctrl+C
CutRight-click → Cut, or Ctrl+X
PasteRight-click → Paste, or Ctrl+V
DeleteRight-click → Move to Trash, or Delete key
RenameRight-click → Rename
New folderRight-click → New Folder

📁 Special Paths

computer:// — Browse the filesystem root. trash:// — View deleted files. Both are accessible from the Files app sidebar.

6. Themes & Appearance

Customize the look of EverestOS from System Settings → Appearance.

Available Themes

🌙 Mint Dark (Default) ☀️ Mint Light ◻️ Minimal 🪟 Windows 95

Appearance Options

SettingDescription
ThemeSwitch between light, dark, minimal, or retro styles
Icon ThemeChoose between Bloom, Bloom Dark, Emoji, or Modern icon sets
Panel OpacityAdjust the transparency of the taskbar panel
Blur EffectEnable or adjust glassmorphic blur intensity
Border RadiusControl the roundness of UI corners
Panel PositionMove the panel to top or bottom of the screen
Night LightApply a warm color filter to reduce eye strain
UI ScalingScale the entire interface (75% to 150%)

7. Keyboard Shortcuts

ShortcutAction
Alt + F2Toggle Looking Glass debug console
Ctrl + ASelect all items (desktop or file manager)
Ctrl + CCopy selected items to clipboard
Ctrl + XCut selected items
Ctrl + VPaste from clipboard
DeleteMove selected items to Trash
EscapeClose dialogs, overlays, and editors

8. Extensions & Plugins

EverestOS supports two types of plugins that integrate into the desktop shell:

Applets (Panel Widgets)

Small tools embedded in the panel/taskbar. Examples include the main menu, date/time display, and system indicators.

  • Add: Right-click panel → "Add Applets"
  • Configure: Right-click the applet → "Settings"
  • Remove: Right-click the applet → "Remove"

Desklets (Desktop Widgets)

Floating widgets on the desktop surface. They can be dragged to any position and persist across sessions.

  • Add: Right-click desktop → "Add Desklets"
  • Move: Drag the desklet by its header
  • Configure: Right-click → "Settings"
  • Remove: Right-click → "Remove this desklet"

🧩 Plugin Directory

All plugins are stored in ~/Plugins/. Applets in ~/Plugins/applets/ and desklets in ~/Plugins/desklets/. You can also manage them from the Plugin Settings app.

9. Configuration

All user settings are stored as JSON files in ~/.config/:

FileWhat It Controls
theme.jsonActive color theme and icon pack
appearance.jsonPanel opacity, blur, border radius
panel.jsonPanel position (top/bottom)
desktop.jsonDesktop icon visibility (Files, Trash, etc.)
display.jsonUI scaling percentage, night light toggle
startup.jsonApps to auto-launch at boot
extensions.jsonWhich extensions are loaded/removed
menu.jsonApp Menu favorites and settings

📝 Editing Config Manually

You can edit these files directly using the Text Editor or Terminal. Changes take effect after refreshing the session.

10. Debugging (Looking Glass)

Press Alt + F2 to open the Looking Glass — an interactive debug console inspired by Cinnamon's diagnostic tool.

  • View logs — System startup messages, extension errors, and app activity
  • Run JavaScript — Execute code directly in the shell context
  • Inspect extensions — See which plugins are loaded and their state
